coredata.dc:title: Waste citrus pectin/garlic bionanohybrids for edible food preservation
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Bowen
Yan, Zhaoxu
Yang, Tianfeng
Zhu, Liying
Wang, Yuxian
Jiang, Ling
coredata.dc:description: Two bioactive molecules derived from citrus peel and garlic (pectin and carbon quantum dot), were used to develop pectin-based edible films for food packaging and coating applications. The interaction between pectin molecules and carbon quantum dots (CDs) resulted in a composite film (CDs/pectin) with improved antioxidant and antibacterial activity, as well as better mechanical properties. The changes brought about by the addition of CDs to the film and their suitability for food packaging were verified through fundamental characterization. The intrinsic fluorescence properties of CDs with pH sensitivity will be further investigated to explore their potential application in food quality detection. Besides, the use of active ingredients from food sources and excellent degradability suggest that the bio-nanocomposite film is a potential edible food packaging material. A formulation based on citrus peel and garlic was used for the surface coating of strawberries to extend their shelf life. The strawberries treated with the active coating did not show any significant signs of decay or dehydration after 5 days. This active packaging coating derived from food ingredients offers a promising natural alternative to conventional materials, with potential applications in extending food product shelf life, promoting human health, and detecting spoilage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Citrus peel
Garlic
Carbon quantum dots (CDs)
Pectin film
food packaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of pectin and CDs/pectin film
Characterization of garlic-derived N, S-co-doped CDs
Appearance analysis of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
Thermal stability of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
Fluorescent properties of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
Physical properties of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
Antioxidant properties of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
Antibacterial activity of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
Shelf life of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite coated strawberries

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Key R&D Program of China ( 2021YFC2102700 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U2106228 ), the Jiangsu Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Bio-Manufacture ( XTC2205 ), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China ( 23KJB550007 ), and the Jiangsu Funding Program for Excellent Postdoctoral Talent ( 2023ZB025 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of garlic-derived CDs. (A) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra at different wavelengths; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra at different pH; (D) HR-TEM images; (E) XPS survey spectrum and the corresponding high-resolution XPS spectrum of (F) C, (G) N, (H) O, and (I) S element.
(A) Real pictures and SEM images of pectin and CDs/pectin film; (B) XRD pattern, (C) FTIR spectrum and (C) EDS mapping of films.
TGA data of pectin and CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film.

(A) Fluorescent properties of films under UV lamp; (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of films; (C) Fluorescence of the composite film after acid immersing; (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of the composite film after acid immersing.
The transmittances of pectin and CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film in different wavelength.
DPPH radical scavenging activity of pectin and CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film.

(A, B) Antibacterial activity of pectin and CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film at 100 and 300 μg/mL (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001); (C) Live/dead bacterial fluorescence staining after pectin and CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film treatment.
(A) Pictures of strawberries stored at room temperature for 5 days; (B) Water loss of preserved strawberries; (C) Mold growth in preserved strawberries (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001).
Physical parameters of pectin and CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film.

head.ce:title: Waste citrus pectin/garlic bionanohybrids for edible food preservation

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Food Science and Light Industry, State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Light Industry
State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two bioactive molecules derived from citrus peel and garlic (pectin and carbon quantum dot), were used to develop pectin-based edible films for food packaging and coating applications. The interaction between pectin molecules and carbon quantum dots (CDs) resulted in a composite film (CDs/pectin) with improved antioxidant and antibacterial activity, as well as better mechanical properties. The changes brought about by the addition of CDs to the film and their suitability for food packaging were verified through fundamental characterization. The intrinsic fluorescence properties of CDs with pH sensitivity will be further investigated to explore their potential application in food quality detection. Besides, the use of active ingredients from food sources and excellent degradability suggest that the bio-nanocomposite film is a potential edible food packaging material. A formulation based on citrus peel and garlic was used for the surface coating of strawberries to extend their shelf life. The strawberries treated with the active coating did not show any significant signs of decay or dehydration after 5 days. This active packaging coating derived from food ingredients offers a promising natural alternative to conventional materials, with potential applications in extending food product shelf life, promoting human health, and detecting spoilage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the Food and Agriculture Organization statistics, approximately 1.3 billion tons of food are wasted per year, accounting for nearly one-third of the global food production (Vilariño et al., 2017; Wunderlich and Martinez, 2018). Previous studies have indicated that the substantial occurrence of food waste is commonly linked to oxidation, foodborne pathogens, and improper storage, leading to the reduction of food quality and shelf life (Abedi-Firoozjah et al., 2023; Umaraw et al., 2020). Apart from the issue of food waste, the potential contamination of foods with pathogenic microorganisms during processing, storage and transportation poses a serious threat to human health (Khaneghah et al., 2020; Rajapaksha et al., 2019). Food packaging plays a crucial role in ensuring the quality and safety of food, as it effectively preserves the freshness and healthiness of food (Bhargava et al., 2020; Brennan et al., 2021). For a long time, the packaging industry has been dominated by plastic packaging due to its excellent performance and convenience (Barone et al., 2021; Kuai et al., 2021). However, on one hand, plastic packing is difficult to degrade in natural environments, resulting in a tremendous environmental burden (Evode et al., 2021; Kedzierski et al., 2020). On the other hand, chemical hazards leaching from plastic will pose a threat to human health (Luo et al., 2022). Therefore, there is a growing demand for contamination-free food packaging and the production of food using sustainable processes that are efficient (Ncube et al., 2020). Recently, more research efforts are focused on the development of biocompatible, biodegradable, and edible food packaging, which holds the potential to extend the shelf life of food and ensure food safety (Manzoor et al., 2023; Thuppahige and Karim, 2022).

Biomacromolecules such as polysaccharides, proteins, and lipids are widely used as recognized biodegradable packaging materials (Chausali et al., 2022). Among them, several natural polysaccharides with excellent film-forming ability are considered to be the ideal candidates, such as chitosan (Shen et al., 2023), sodium alginate (Saravanakumar et al., 2020), starch (Vianna et al., 2021), pectin (Priyadarshi et al., 2021), xanthan gum (Chen et al., 2022). Edible coating based on pectin, one of the byproduct materials of agriculture and food industry, has been of great interest in preserving foods because of its abundance, low cost, ease of isolation, non-toxicity and polymerizing properties, which is mainly extracted from waste peels of citrus fruits and apple pomace (Espitia et al., 2014; Kumar et al., 2020). Abundant research has substantiated the potential application of pectin-based composites as coatings for food packaging (Valdés et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2019). However, the poor mechanical properties and high hydrophilicity of single-component pectin films, resulting in the sensitivity to moisture and poor water vapor permeability, have limited its application in packaging (Lazaridou and Biliaderis, 2020; Ren et al., 2022).

Recently, several researches had combined two or more components to construct nanocomposite films with enhanced performance in package film (Makaremi et al., 2019). Lin et al. (2020) reported an active edible composite film composed of soluble dietary fiber, carboxymethyl cellulose, thyme essential oil, and pectin. The addition of pectin resulted in a significant improvement of mechanical and optical properties, with tensile strength of 21.419 ± 2.22 MPa, and the minimum transparency of 88.9 ± 0.42%. Fan et al. (2021) developed an edible composite food packaging based on pectin, which exhibited good water vapor transmission rate (18.12 × 10−11 g/(m2·s·pa)), as well as preservation for fresh-cut potatoes. Besides, many synthetic and inorganic nanoparticles were considered suitable for use as preservatives in biopolymeric food packaging films, such as Ag NPs, TiO2, CuONPs, and ZnONPs (Zhang et al., 2023). Shankar et al. (2016) reported a pectin-based nanocomposite film with excellent physical properties through incorporation of Ag NPs. Additionally, metal oxides have demonstrated significant bioactivity in the design of food packaging because of their antibacterial effect, oxygen and ethylene scavenging ability, and thermal stability (Nikolic et al., 2021; Zarandona et al., 2023). Although nanoparticles could endow the films with improved mechanical properties, antibacterial and antioxidant activities, inorganic ions in nanoparticles might migrate from films to foods, thus exerting a serious threat to human health (Liang et al., 2023). Therefore, exploring multifunctional nanoparticles with high safety and good biocompatibility is the pioneer of a true edible nanocomposite packaging film.

Recently, some studies have revealed the strong antibacterial and potent antioxidant activity of carbon quantum dots (CDs), which have become a desirable choice for active packaging applications because of their small size (<10 nm) and unique optical functional properties (Algarra et al., 2020; Ezati et al., 2022). Ezati et al. (2022b) prepared a pectin/gelatin-based bioactive food packaging film containing CDs, and concluded that sulfur-functionalized CDs composite films exhibited excellent microbial protection and protection against food oxidative activity, high UV protection effect, and superior mechanical, water vapor permeability, and hydrophobicity properties. Inspired by it, in this work, a fluorescent N, S-co-doped CDs was prepared using dietary organosulfur from garlic as a green source by one-step hydrothermal synthesis. Then, CDs were incorporated into pectin-based films as functional material for active packaging. The properties of UV-light protection, mechanical, water vapor permeability, antioxidant activity, and antibacterial effect were investigated in details. Finally, the preservation efficacy of the films was assessed by applying an edible coating to strawberries, aiming to prolong the shelf life of the food product. This work will provide the perspective of applying value-added food sources as sustainable and inexpensive multifunctional additives in food packaging to impart functional properties.

The oranges, garlic and strawberries were purchased from local supermarket (Nanjing, China). Sodium citrate, ethanol, methanol, glycerol, and d-glucose were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Agar, tryptone and yeast extract were purchased from OXOID Ltd (UK). And other reagents were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). Ultrapure water with a specific resistance of 18.2 MΩ was used throughout the experiment. Salmonella typhimurium ATCC 14028 was purchased from Beinuo Biology (Shanghai, China).

The citrus peels were obtained from oranges, and they were dried in the oven at 101 °C overnight. The dried citrus peel was ground into a powder and dispersed in a sodium citrate buffer solution with a pH of 4 to prepare a 5 wt% mixed solution. The solution was then rapidly stirred at 70 °C for 3 h, and the supernatant was extracted and mixed with three times its volume of anhydrous ethanol. After stirring for 10 min, the resulting mixture was precipitated at 4 °C, and the solid obtained was identified as crude pectin (Wang et al., 2021).

A mass of 500 g fresh garlic was cut into small pieces and placed directly into a reactor at 220 °C for 6 h. Then, the garlic cloves were processed into black powder. The powder was then mixed with 500 mL of water and stirred for 24 h. Finally, the unreacted solid impurities were removed, and the resulting supernatant was a water-soluble solution containing CDs (Sun et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pectin and CDs/pectin film
ce:section.ce:para: The crude pectin (3 g) extracted from citrus peel was dissolved in water (100 mL) to create a 3% aqueous solution, which was then heated to 50 °C while stirring. Once the pectin was completely dissolved, 2% of glycerol was added as a plasticizer. Approximately, 15 mL of the resulting crude pectin solution was poured into a circular plastic mold with a diameter of 8 cm. Subsequently, it was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 3 h, allowing the formation of a dry solid pectin film. For the preparation of pectin film containing CDs, all the process was the same except for adding CDs solution. Namely, 3 g of crude pectin was first dissolved in 50 mL of water, and then mixed with 50 mL CDs aqueous solution at 50 °C until the pectin was fully dissolved and the CDs were evenly dispersed.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of CDs was obtained using the Lambda 25 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured on a SpectraMax M3 (Molecular Devices, USA) microplate reader by changing the excitation wavelength (300–400 nm) and pH of buffer solution (3–10). The morphology of CDs was observed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2100 F, Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo, USA) analysis was conducted to determine the elemental composition and confirm the nitrogen and sulfur functionalization of CDs. The surface morphology of films was analyzed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Quanta FEG 250, FEI, USA). For this, all film samples were cut into square pieces with a surface area of 0.25π cm2, then coated with a layer of gold power to enhance their conductivity and placed on a layer of conductive adhesives to observe the microstructure (Shen et al., 2023). The element distribution of film was detected by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The chemical structure and crystal phase of dried film powder were analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS500, Thermo, USA) and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, MiniFlex 600, Rigaku, Japan), respectively.

Water vapor permeability (WVP) of films was measured by gravimetrically methodology according to ASTM E00996-00 procedure (Yadav et al., 2021). The entirely dried silica gel was used, and the films pattern were cut and wrapped over a glass cup for each measurement. The cup containing distilled water was placed on a desiccator, and the weight changes were recorded at 4 h interval for 3 consecutive days at room temperature. The WVP (g·m−1·h−1·Pa−1) was calculated according to the following equation:
W a t e r
v a p o r
p e r m e a b i l i t y =
W × x
t × A × Δ P
where W is weight gain of film sample (g), x is the thickness of the film (m), t is the time for weight change of the cup (h), A is the film permeation area (m2), and △P is the difference in partial vapor pressure across two sides of the film.

The film thickness was measured using a thickness gauge, and each value was expressed as an average of five measurements. The tensile strength and elongation at break (EBA) were measured using an electronic tensiometer. Before the measurement, the films were cut into rectangular strips in 5 cm length and 0.5 cm width. Afterwards, the strips were affixed to the electronic tensiometer. Subsequently, the films were slowly stretched at a consistent rate until it reached the point of rupture, during which time recorded the length and tension of the films. This process was repeated three times for each type of film.

To determine the initial dry mass (W1), film portions (0.25π mm2) were dried at 80 °C for 12 h in the oven. Then, the portions of the films were stored for 24 h in 100 mL beakers containing 50 mL of water. Finally, the films were dried in the oven and subsequently weighted to obtain the final dry mass (W2). The following equation was used to calculate the water solubility of films:
W a t e r
S o l u b i l i t y
( % )
=
W 1
−
W 2
W 1
× 100

The UV–Vis light transmittance of the films was measured on a Lambda 25 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) at wavelength from 400 to 1000 nm, and the opacity was calculated using the absorbance at 600 nm (Abs600) according to the equation: opacity = Abs
600
/d, where d was the film thickness (mm).

The antioxidant activity of pectin films was evaluated by assaying the scavenging of free radical of DPPH according to the literature with some modification (Yadav et al., 2021). Briefly, different weight film samples (100, 200, and 300 μg) with a surface area of 0.25π cm2 were added into 1 mL of DPPH methanol solution (0.25 mM). After shaken in an oscillator, the mixture was allowed to react in dark at room temperature for 60 min. The UV absorbance of DPPH solution at 517 nm was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The antioxidant activity was calculated using the following equation:
A n t i o x i d a n t
a c t i v i t y
( % )
=
Abs
( blank )
− Abs
( sample )
Abs
( blank )
× 100

The antibacterial assay of the films was studied by colony counting method against Salmonella typhimurium in solid agar media. Firstly, Salmonella typhimurium bacteria was cultured in 50 mL of Luria-Bertani (LB) medium at 37 °C for 12 h. Then, different weight film samples (100 and 300 μg) were added into 1 mL of bacterial suspension with a concentration of 107–108 CFU. After 30 min incubation of the mixture, 100 μL of the suspension was serially diluted three times and plated onto LB agar medium for colony counting at 37 °C for 12 h (Shen et al., 2022, 2023). The live/dead bacterial fluorescence staining was conducted using SYTO 9 and propidium iodide (PI) according to the protocol of the manufacture, and the fluorescence images were observed with a fluorescence microscope (Olympus IX71, Olympus, Japan).

Fresh strawberries were used to evaluate the preservative effect of films by coating method. Before the assay, the strawberries were thoroughly washed, dried, and then stored indoors. Afterwards, the strawberries were immersed in the pectin mixture solution (3% w/v) with or without CDs for 2 min, and immediately removed and hung on the apparatus for drying. This treatment resulted in the formation of film coating on the surface of the strawberries, which were then stored indoors alongside the untreated strawberries at room temperature for 5 days. At least five replicates were performed for each experiment. The surface changes of all strawberries were recorded daily by photography, and their weight was measured daily to track moisture loss. Finally, each strawberry was submerged in 20 mL of PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.0) to collect surface microorganisms, and the resulting solution was transferred to LB agar plates to calculate the CFUs of the microorganism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of garlic-derived N, S-co-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were widely used in anti-counterfeiting and smart packaging due to the special luminescence property (Zhao et al., 2022). Therefore, the optical performance of as-prepared CDs was investigated through UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the UV–Vis spectra showed an obvious optical absorption peak mainly at 280 nm (UV region), which probably originated from the π–π* electron transition of nanocarbon (Zhang et al., 2014). The CDs exhibited a maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 365 and 450 nm, respectively, as revealed by the photoluminescence spectra. Meanwhile, the color of CDs aqueous solution changed from dark yellow to strong cyan luminescence under daylight and UV light irradiation, respectively (insets in Fig. 1A), further visually exhibiting its fluorescence characteristic. Fig. 1B depicts the emission spectra of CDs as they varied with different excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 400 nm. It could be seen that CDs with different excitation wavelengths exhibited sharp emission peaks under exposure to excitation wavelength at 300–400 nm, with the highest peak located at 360 nm, which is twice as high as that at 300 nm. Further changing the excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 400 resulted in the decrease of emission spectra, as well as the shift towards long wavelengths from 450 nm to 500 nm, which was consistent with most of fluorescent CDs reported before. This red shift phenomenon may be attributed to the anti-Stokes photoluminescence property and the complexity of different surfaces states in CDs (Li et al., 2021). Besides, the emission spectra of CDs could also be changed by environmental factors. Therefore, the influence of various pH on fluorescence property was explored for a wide range of pH from 3 to 10. In Fig. 1C, the results reveal an enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs as the pH increased from acidic (pH 3) to alkaline conditions (pH 10). This pH-dependent photoluminescence behavior was attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of amino groups on the surface of CDs (Yang et al., 2019), which could be utilized as a sensor to monitor pH changes and detect alkaline biogenic amine during food preservation (Khan et al., 2023).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was employed to characterize the morphology and size of the obtained CDs. The CDs presented spherical and uniform shapes with good dispersibility (Fig. 1D). The average size of the CDs was calculated and found to be about 3.5 nm, which is in good concurrence with ultra-small size of typical CDs (Gupta et al., 2020). The elemental composition and surface functional groups of CDs were further determined by XPS. The survey spectrum given in Fig. 1E demonstrates two strong peaks at 284 and 532 eV, as well as two weak peaks at 350 and 170 eV, corresponding to the C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 1s, respectively, indicating the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups and nitrogen-containing species in CDs. These elemental peaks were deconvoluted into several distinctive peaks, illustrating the surface functional groups. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, it could be split into three characteristic peaks located at 284.61, 287.70, and 285.90 eV, confirming the existence of CC, C–O, and C–N/C–H, respectively (Fig. 1F) (Alas et al., 2022). Similarly, the high-resolution scan of O1s exhibited three peaks at 531.02 and 532.69 eV, which correspond to the CO and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively (Fig. 1H) (Mei et al., 2022). Additionally, other fitted peaks of N 1s and S 2p peaks at 399.27, 168.12, and 169.40 eV corresponded to the pyridinic-like N for N 1s, and S–O3, S–O4 chemical bond for S 2p (Fig. 1G and I) (Ezati et al., 2022b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Appearance analysis of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
ce:section.ce:para: The appearance of the different film components can be observed in Fig. 2 A. The pectin extracted from citrus peel exhibited properties similar to commercial pectin (Fig. S1). The film prepared from pectin appeared transparent, while the composite film with the addition of CDs was a dark brown transparent film (Fig. 2A). The microstructure of pectin-based films was further observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The neat pectin film exhibited a homogeneous, crack-free, and dense surface. After the addition of CDs to the polymer matrix, the composite film showed a slightly rougher surface than the neat film, indicating the aggregation in the film, which is corresponding to other reported CDs-doped packaging films. The elemental mapping in Fig. 2D determined the elemental composition of composite film. The mapping images revealed the presence of C, O, and N elements in the pectin film. In addition to C, O, and N elements, the composite film exhibited the unique presence of S elements derived from the CDs.

The XRD analysis in Fig. 2B was conducted to determine crystallinity of films. Apparently, the characteristic peak of the diffraction pattern was observed at 2θ of 30.6° for both the neat and CDs-doped films, which was attributed to the crystalline phase of pectin. Furthermore, the chemical bonding and compatibility between pectin and CDs were studied using FTIR analysis. As shown in Fig. 2C, CDs exhibited a sharp peak at 3100-3500 cm−1, which attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H, and the observation of peaks at 1637, 1618, 1400, and 1102 cm−1 suggested the presence of stretching vibration of CO, CC, C–C, and C–O bonds, respectively, which was also observed in pectin film (Sharaby et al., 2022). Besides, the characteristic bands observed at 3308 and 2903 cm−1 in the film were assigned to the stretching vibrations of –CH3 and –CH2 groups in pectin, respectively. For the physical mixture of CDs and pectin film, the addition of CDs did not result in the formation of new functional groups, indicating that the interaction between CDs and pectin primarily involves physical interaction. The study has demonstrated that nanoparticles tend to fill the gaps in the polymer, which helps to improve the density and physical properties of the film, making it more suitable for applications in food preservation (Shen et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal characteristics of neat pectin film and composite film were investigated using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). As depicted in Fig. 3 , all films underwent two distinct periods of noticeable mass loss. The first weight loss of about 5% occurred till 80 °C, which is attributed to the loss of non-crystalline water in the films. The second prominent thermal degradation was observed around 180–280 °C, with maximum weight loss (65–70%) caused by pectin backbone decomposition. Notably, TGA curve for CDs-doped film exhibited smaller weight change compared to the neat film, indicating great thermal stability of CDs-doped composite film (Singh et al., 2019). The final residual char content of films showed somewhat low values, illustrating the purity of pectin and content of CDs (∼5%). The thermal gravimetric analysis data indicated the successful formation of the composite film and its superior thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent properties of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the pH-sensitive fluorescence properties of garlic-derived CDs, there is potential for their application in food quality monitoring. When food begins to decay and produces acidic substances that are not visible to the naked eye, these acidic volatiles can interact with the pH-sensitive CDs. These interactions cause a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs, making them visible as a marker for food spoilage. Therefore, the fluorescence property of the composite film was further investigated to examine their feasibility for food quality monitoring. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, the composite film exhibited fluorescence under UV light, which was not observed in the pure pectin film. The interaction between CDs and pectin molecules did not compromise the fluorescence properties of CDs, making them suitable for application in food quality monitoring. Subsequently, we exposed the surface of the composite film to the phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with a pH of 4 and allowed it to naturally evaporate. Interestingly, a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed at the location where the PBS evaporated from the surface of the composite film (Fig. 4C and D). This indicates the potential application of the fluorescence properties conferred by CDs in the composite film for food quality monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical properties of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
Antioxidant properties of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant effect of packaging film is crucial in preventing the oxidative deterioration of food. It has been reported that the doping of CDs endows the film with excellent antioxidant activity (Fu et al., 2022). The antioxidant activity of the CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film was measured by the scavenging of DPPH radicals, and the results can be observed in Fig. 6 . As is evident from the figure, the DPPH scavenging ability increased with the addition of films. The maximum scavenging activity of neat pectin film was only about 25% at 300 μg/mL. In contrast, CDs-doped pectin film showed significant DPPH scavenging activity with an increase in the concentration. Almost 50% free radical was scavenged at 300 μg/mL of film, showing an enhancement in the antioxidant property of bio-nanocomposite film. The high antioxidant activity was due to the presence of surface hydroxyl groups of CDs, which lead to the reduction and quenching of DPPH· by H· transferring from hydroxyl groups of the CDs (Roy et al., 2021). This indicates that the addition of CDs makes the bio-nanocomposite film possess better antioxidant capability, which can help prevent food oxidation in terms of food preservation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film
ce:section.ce:para: Apart from antioxidant activity, prevention of food contamination by bacteria presents a significant challenge in food packaging. Packaging materials with broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties are advantageous for prolonging food storage. Here, the antibacterial activity of films against the typical foodborne bacterium Salmonella typhimurium was evaluated qualitatively by plate counting method. As shown in Fig. 7 A, both neat and CDs-doped pectin film exhibited certain degree of antibacterial effect. The antibacterial activity of films composed of various components showed a dependence on concentration, with an increase in antibacterial activity as concentration increased. The CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film showed enhanced antibacterial effect, with survival rate of 20 and 5% at different concentration, respectively (Fig. 7B). The results indicated that CDs synthesized from garlic endow the film with superior antibacterial effect. Further confirmation of bacterial apoptosis was performed through bacterial live/dead fluorescence staining. The bacteria were stained with both propidium iodide (PI) and SYTO 9, displaying red fluorescence (labeling dead cells) and green fluorescence (labeling all bacteria whether they are alive or dead). As shown in Fig. 7C, almost no apoptotic bacteria were observed in the control group. A small number of apoptotic bacteria were observed in the pectin group, while large number of apoptotic bacteria were observed in the CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite group. This suggests that the addition of CDs endowed the pectin membrane with enhanced antibacterial capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Shelf life of CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite coated strawberries
ce:section.ce:para: In order to practically assess whether the CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film has the potential for food preservation, the suitability of the coating was evaluated by applying it to strawberry fruits to verify its effectiveness in food preservation. Fresh strawberries were coated with neat and CDs-doped pectin film, and stored at room temperature for 5 days. The changes in the appearance during storage are shown in Fig. 8 A. The strawberries showed a fresh appearance in the first two days. However, the control group (without any coating) started to deteriorate after 3 days, while the pectin film coated strawberries remained fresh appearance. After 5 days storage, the uncoated strawberries presented a serious deteriorated, with mold growth becoming apparent on their surface. For neat pectin film coated strawberries, the surface became gloomy, and partly deteriorated. Notably, strawberries coated with CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film maintained their original freshness and color, and no mold growth was observed on their surface (Fig. 8B). The water loss and contamination of microorganisms further verified the good preservation ability of coating films (Fig. 8B and C). The CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film could significantly retard the loss of water in strawberries, and prevent contamination of strawberries by foodborne microorganisms. Therefore, this nanocomposite film demonstrates promising potential as an active edible coating for foods, offering the ability to extend their shelf life, preserve their quality during preservation.

This study successfully developed a bioactive edible film using pectin rich in active compounds in citrus peel and garlic as raw materials. The incorporation of garlic-derived CDs into the film significantly improved its mechanical and light-blocking properties, as confirmed by the characterization of interaction between CDs and pectin. Additionally, the pH sensitivity of CDs derived from garlic suggests the potential of the bio-nanocomposite film as a smart food quality detection packaging. The bio-nanocomposite film also demonstrated remarkable thermal stability, antioxidant and antibacterial properties, making it a promising candidate for food packaging and coating applications. Specifically, the CDs/pectin bio-nanocomposite film was found to extend the shelf life of strawberries by preventing food decay and controlling food moisture loss, thus providing a potential solution for food packaging and storage. Notably, the developed edible coating was also effective in preventing surface mold growth on food. In conclusion, the findings of this study provide compelling evidence for the potential of the composite film derived from citrus peel and garlic as a promising natural alternative for food packaging in the food industry.

Bowen Shen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Zhaoxu Yan: Formal analysis, Data curation. Tianfeng Yang: Formal analysis, Methodology. Liying Zhu: Supervision, Project administration. Yuxian Wang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Investigation. Ling Jiang: Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Key R&D Program of China (2021YFC2102700), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U2106228), the Jiangsu Synergetic Innovation Center for Advanced Bio-Manufacture (XTC2205), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (23KJB550007), and the Jiangsu Funding Program for Excellent Postdoctoral Talent (2023ZB025).
